1.exposition: The high Christology of the first two chapters now becomes a summons. Drawing his "wherefore" from all that has been shown, the writer addresses the readers as holy brethren, partakers of a heavenly calling, and bids them fix the attentive eye of faith on "the Apostle and High Priest of our confession, Jesus" — the one sent from God to us, and the one who goes from us to God, two offices meeting in a single person. Then comes a second and, for a Jewish reader, weightier comparison than the angels: Moses. The writer grants Moses his glory outright — faithful in all God's house — before he surpasses it. Christ is counted worthy of more glory, as the builder of a house has more honour than the house; and since the builder of all things is God, the One who built the house is no member of it like Moses but its divine Maker. The distinction is sealed in the last two verses: Moses was faithful in the house as a servant; Christ is faithful over the house as a Son — and we are that house, if we hold our confidence fast to the end.
1.original_language: The governing verb is katanoēsate (v.1), "consider" — literally to put the mind down upon a thing (kata + nous), to fix and hold the thought. Two titles follow: apostolos ("one sent," applied to Jesus only here in the New Testament) and archiereus ("high priest") — the two directions of mediation named at once. Moses is God's therapōn in the Septuagint of Numbers 12:7 — an honoured attendant, yet a servant — while Christ is huios, Son. The faith they profess is their homologia ("confession"), a word that tolls through the letter as a call to hold fast (4:14; 10:23).
1.historical_context: No human figure stood higher in Second-Temple Jewish piety than Moses; Philo devoted two whole books to his life, portraying him as legislator, prophet, and priest. The cornerstone of that honour was the title God himself gave him — "faithful in all my house" (Numbers 12:7) — the very text that established a hierarchy of revelation, God speaking to Moses mouth to mouth while other prophets received only visions and dreams. To measure Jesus against Moses was therefore the boldest claim a writer could make to such an audience, and the household analogy of these verses draws its very terms — house, servant, faithful — straight from that Numbers passage.
1.christ: The double title of v.1 is unique to Hebrews and christologically dense: as Apostle, Jesus pleads God's cause with us; as High Priest, he pleads our cause with God — the two movements of divine-human communication meeting in one person. The quiet climax is v.4: every house has a builder, but the builder of all things is God — and Christ has just been named the builder of the house, so the maxim carries the weight of his deity. Moses is a servant in the house; the Son is Lord over it, because the house is his own.

verses.note: The letter's first direct appeal to its readers: consider the Apostle and High Priest of our confession, Jesus. grammatical-historical Robertson notes that apostolos is applied to Jesus nowhere else in the New Testament, and that these Hebrew Christians had already "confessed" (homologia) him in both offices — the word a standing summons to fidelity (4:14). puritan Matthew Henry marks how seldom even holy brethren truly "consider" Christ, and how much such fixed thought would deepen love and obedience. grammatical-historical Ellicott, following Bengel, sets the two titles side by side: as Apostle he pleads God's cause with us, as High Priest he pleads our cause with God. grammatical-historical Gill adds that "brethren" names not natural lineage but a spiritual relation — the same Father, the adopted sons of God.

The comparison opens on common ground: Christ was faithful to him that appointed him, as also Moses was faithful in all his house. grammatical-historical Gill is careful with "appointed" (or "made") him — the phrase concerns the constituting of Christ in his office as Mediator, not his divine nature, which was never made. The likeness is granted before the superiority is shown, echoing the very words God spoke of Moses (Numbers 12:7). puritan Matthew Henry presses the force of it for a Jewish reader: they prized the faithfulness of Moses, yet that faithfulness was itself only a type of Christ's, who is sealed and sent by the Father and answers fully to his trust.

Now the greater glory: this man was counted worthy of more glory than Moses, inasmuch as he who built the house has more honour than the house. grammatical-historical Gill grants Moses real glory, given him both by God and by men in the work he was called to; but the architect is honoured above the building he raises. puritan Matthew Henry sharpens the categories: Moses was a member and a minister in the house, whereas Christ is its maker — and to build the church took no less power than to build the world, the one framed out of nothing, the other out of materials wholly unfit for such a house.

A maxim that carries a hidden argument: every house is built by someone, but he that built all things is God. grammatical-historical Gill hears the syllogism beneath the surface — a house does not build itself; the universal Builder, who frames the whole creation and the whole church, is God; and Christ has just been named the builder of God's house. The unspoken conclusion is his deity: the One who builds the house is no creature like Moses but God himself, the maker of all. What looks like a passing observation is the pivot on which the whole comparison turns.

The precise nature of Moses' faithfulness, stated so as to set it below Christ's: Moses was faithful in all his house as a servant, for a testimony of those things which were to be spoken after. grammatical-historical Gill notes Moses was truly the Lord's servant — of God's choosing, sending, and approving — and faithful in his charge; but faithful "as a servant," in the rank and place of a servant, not the lord. And his very ministry was prophetic: all his service pointed forward, witnessing beforehand to the gospel and to the Christ who was to come. Moses' honour was the honour of a faithful servant testifying to another greater than himself.

The crown of the comparison, and its turn upon the reader: Christ as a son over his own house; whose house are we, if we hold fast the confidence and the rejoicing of the hope firm unto the end. grammatical-historical Gill marks that Christ is Son not by creation, adoption, or office but by nature, the only begotten — and so Lord and master over the house, which Moses never was. On the "if," reformed Calvin and puritan Matthew Henry agree it makes salvation depend on nothing in us: it describes the true members of the house, who are known by a confidence and hope held firm to the end — and Henry adds that such perseverance is itself the fruit of being "kept by the mighty power of God." The condition already leans toward the warning that follows.

witnesses.point: "Him that appointed him" concerns Christ's office, not his divine nature; and Christ is Son over the house by nature, Lord where Moses was only a servant.
Moses was a minister within the house, but Christ is its maker and master — worthy of more glory and of closer, more serious consideration.
The twin titles answer Moses and Aaron: the offices those two divided — teacher-messenger and priest — are united in Christ the one Mediator.
Following Bengel: as Apostle, Jesus pleads God's cause with us; as High Priest, he pleads our cause with God.

external.name: F.F. Bruce
external.point: The contrast honours Moses rather than diminishing him: his faithfulness is genuine, but exercised as a servant within the very household over which the Son presides as builder and heir.

1.application: Consider him. The household that is Christ's is not entered by a moment's profession and then forgotten, but held — a confidence and a rejoicing hope kept firm to the end. Fix the mind where the writer points it: on the Son who is both the word God sends to us and the priest who carries us to God.

7.pericope_label: Warning: Do Not Harden Your Hearts
7.exposition: To enforce the perseverance just urged — "if we hold fast" — the writer reaches for Scripture, and the Scripture he reaches for is Psalm 95, ascribed not to David but to the Holy Ghost speaking now: Today, if you hear his voice, harden not your hearts. He recites Israel's wilderness rebellion — the provocation and testing at Meribah and Massah, forty years of tempting God while watching his works — until he swore in his wrath, "They shall not enter my rest." Then the warning lands on the readers: take heed lest an evil, unbelieving heart turn any of you away from the living God; exhort one another daily, while it is still "Today," lest sin's deceit harden you by unfelt degrees. The positive counterpart is union: we are partakers of Christ, if we hold our first confidence firm to the end. Finally a chain of three questions drives the lesson home — who provoked God? whose bodies fell? whom did the oath exclude? — and the answer, each time, narrows to one thing: they could not enter in because of unbelief.
7.original_language: The pulse of the passage is sēmeron, "Today," struck three times (v.7, v.13, v.15) — the present hour of opportunity. Against it stands sklērunō, "harden," the stiffening of a heart into stone. grammatical-historical Ellicott presses the grammar the English blurs: the Greek is "if ye shall hear his voice" (ean akousēte), a real condition, not "if ye are willing to hear" — and elsewhere Scripture makes the hardening a man's own act (Exodus 9:34; 1 Samuel 6:6; Proverbs 28:14). "Provocation" and "temptation" render the place-names Meribah and Massah (Exodus 17:7), and katapausis, "rest," is the word the next chapter will pry wide open (4:1). The section closes on the single deadly word apistia — unbelief.
7.historical_context: Psalm 95 — the Venite — was sung at daily worship, so this warning was already woven into the readers' own prayers. Its scene is the wilderness: Israel redeemed from Egypt but not yet in Canaan, testing God at Meribah and Massah (Exodus 17:7), murmuring for forty years until that whole generation's carcasses fell in the desert (Numbers 14:29). eastern Chrysostom counts three rests in view across the argument — the Sabbath rest in which God ceased from his works, the rest of Canaan, and the true rest that is the kingdom of heaven. The writer reads the Psalm's "Today" not as a word to a dead past but as living address to the present, the same actualizing move by which the Qumran interpreters heard ancient texts speaking directly to their own community.
7.christ: The "rest" the wilderness generation forfeited (v.11) proves, as the next chapter argues, to be not finally Canaan but God's own Sabbath rest — still open (4:1) and entered by faith. To "turn away from the living God" (v.12) is, in this letter, to turn from the Son in whom God has spoken his last word. And the warning's positive side is union with Christ himself: we are partakers of Christ — sharers in him and all he is — the very substance of salvation. The wilderness generation stands as the negative type; the readers, by holding fast to Christ, become the antitype that generation could only foreshadow.

verses.note: The quotation opens with a weighty note of its Author: Wherefore, as the Holy Ghost saith, Today if ye will hear his voice. eastern Chrysostom marks the flow — having urged us to hold our hope firm, the writer now proves from Scripture that we must look forward with steadiness, and he ascribes the Psalm's words expressly to the Holy Ghost, the very voice of God. grammatical-historical Robertson, following Moffatt, reads the "wherefore" with "harden not" in the next verse, the long citation (Psalm 95:7–11) standing as a parenthesis before the author unfolds it in three movements (3:12–19; 4:1–10; 4:11–13). grammatical-historical Barnes takes "Today" to mean simply now: God's commands touch the present moment, never tomorrow.

Harden not your hearts, as in the provocation, in the day of temptation in the wilderness. grammatical-historical Gill distinguishes a natural hardness — the heart of man like a stone, on which no impression can be made but by powerful grace — from an acquired, habitual hardness gathered by sinning. The charge is not to stiffen the heart against God's voice by unbelief, "as in the provocation," that is, at Meribah and Massah (Exodus 17:7), where the fathers dared God. puritan Matthew Henry observes that days of temptation are often days of provocation, and to provoke God while wholly living upon him is provocation with a witness.

The aggravation of the sin: when your fathers tempted me, proved me, and saw my works forty years. grammatical-historical Gill notes they put God to the trial by repeated unbelief and murmuring, as if doubting his power and faithfulness — and did so while beholding his mighty works, the daily manna, the cloud, the deliverances, for forty long years. Their unbelief was the more inexcusable for being sinned against the clearest and most prolonged evidence of God's presence and power that any people ever saw.

God's own verdict on the generation: Wherefore I was grieved with that generation, and said, They do alway err in their heart; and they have not known my ways. grammatical-historical Gill notes the language of grief is used after the manner of men — God displeased and offended — and the ground is named twice: their straying was not occasional but constant and inward, a habitual erring of the heart itself, and they refused to learn God's ways. puritan Matthew Henry finds here the spring of all the rest: their heart-errors bred the errors of their lips and lives, and a settled estrangement of the heart was the root of their ruin.

The dreadful issue: So I sware in my wrath, They shall not enter into my rest. grammatical-historical Gill notes that swearing is ascribed to God to show the certainty of the thing — as he swears in love to confirm mercy, so here in wrath to confirm judgment, and the threatened evil is made irrevocable. The rest is first the land of Canaan, but eastern Chrysostom sees beyond it three rests — Sabbath, Canaan, and the kingdom of heaven — so the oath shuts the faithless out of far more than a country. The carcasses of that generation fell in the wilderness (Numbers 14:29), never reaching the promise.

The application, pressed home with affection: Take heed, brethren, lest there be in any of you an evil heart of unbelief, in departing from the living God. grammatical-historical Barnes draws the greater danger — because Christ so far surpasses Moses, apostasy from him is a graver peril than any threatened under the old economy. grammatical-historical Gill reads the phrase as a single movement: the heart is evil, therefore unbelieving, therefore it departs — and the God it departs from is "the living God," no dead idol from whom turning is harmless, but the God whose judgment is as certain as his life. puritan Matthew Henry names unbelief the first step to apostasy: let him that thinks he stands take heed lest he fall.

The remedy is not solitary vigilance but the mutual care of the body: exhort one another daily, while it is called To day; lest any of you be hardened through the deceitfulness of sin. puritan Matthew Henry dwells on that deceitfulness — sin appears fair but is filthy, promises much and performs nothing, and hardens the soul by slow, unfelt degrees, one allowed sin preparing the way for the next until sinning against conscience sears it. grammatical-historical Gill marks the daily, brotherly exhortation as God's own appointed means to keep any from being so deceived, while the present season of "Today" still lasts.

A further motive to steadfastness: we are made partakers of Christ, if we hold the beginning of our confidence stedfast unto the end. grammatical-historical Gill reads "partakers of Christ" as union with him — sharers in his person, grace, righteousness, and life. On the "if," the witnesses divide honestly. reformed Calvin and puritan Matthew Henry take it not as the cause by which we become partakers but as the proof of it — persevering faith is the best evidence of a sincere faith, and those truly Christ's are kept to the end by the power of God. wesleyan-arminian Wesley and Clarke read the same "if," and all the letter's warnings, as evidence that the grace of God can be finally lost, and that perseverance is a real duty on which the promise hangs. The text gives both their foothold: a genuine condition, held out to people who are urged to meet it.

The Spirit's word is pressed once more: While it is said, To day if ye will hear his voice, harden not your hearts, as in the provocation. grammatical-historical Gill notes the repetition is made to draw a further improvement from it — the very fact that Scripture still says "Today" shows the warning is no dead letter of the past but a living word of present concern to the gospel hearers. grammatical-historical Ellicott recovers the grammar the English loses: the Greek is "if ye shall hear his voice," a true condition, not "if ye are willing" — and the hardening is repeatedly named in Scripture as a man's own act (Exodus 9:34; Proverbs 28:14).

The first of three questions: For some, when they had heard, did provoke: howbeit not all that came out of Egypt by Moses. grammatical-historical Gill draws the sharp point — the very people who had heard God's voice and word were the ones who rebelled, so that hearing alone does not save. Yet "not all," for there were exceptions: Caleb and Joshua believed. The mass of hearers fell by provoking unbelief, while a faithful few were preserved. puritan Matthew Henry adds that God will always have a remnant obedient to his voice, and takes care of them and mentions them with honour.

The second question: But with whom was he grieved forty years? was it not with them that had sinned, whose carcases fell in the wilderness? grammatical-historical Barnes asks plainly with whom God was angry, and answers: with those who had sinned in various ways — by rebellion, murmuring, and unbelief — whose dead bodies were strewn across the desert (Numbers 14:29). The visible proof of God's long displeasure was a whole generation perishing in the wilderness, never reaching the rest.

The third and climactic question narrows the cause of their fall to its root: And to whom sware he that they should not enter into his rest, but to them that believed not? grammatical-historical Barnes fixes the precise sin — they "did not confide in God," and for that want of faith he solemnly swore they should not enter. Of all the ways they had sinned, the one against which the oath was sworn, the one that shut them out of the rest, was unbelief — singled out as the deadly, excluding sin, the very thing the Hebrews are being warned to shun.

The whole argument gathered into one conclusion: So we see that they could not enter in because of unbelief. grammatical-historical Gill notes the Old Testament's own testimony shows plainly that it was unbelief, and nothing else, that barred that generation from God's rest, their carcasses falling in the wilderness before they came to Canaan. This is the great practical point of the passage — unbelief is the fatal, excluding sin — and the warning to the Hebrews, and to all, is to beware of that same unbelief, lest they too come short of the rest that yet remains (4:1).

witnesses.point: The Psalm's words are the Holy Spirit's own; three rests are in view — Sabbath, Canaan, and the kingdom of heaven — and the true reward of those who endure lies in the last.
"Today" means now, touching the present moment; and unbelief — the failure to confide in God — is the one sin against which the oath of exclusion was sworn.
The hardening of the heart is the spring of all other sins, and sin hardens by slow degrees — so believers must exhort one another daily while it is still Today.
"Seeing he is faithful, be not ye unfaithful": the warnings show perseverance to be a real duty and the grace of God something that can, by unbelief, be finally lost.

external.name: William L. Lane
external.point: The exhortation is corporate: the daily, mutual encouragement of the community is the God-given safeguard against the hardening that Psalm 95's "Today" warns of, with the wilderness generation set as the typological negative example.

7.application: "Today" is the operative word. The door of the rest still stands open, and the deceit of sin still works by unfelt degrees — which is why the writer prescribes not private resolve but daily, mutual encouragement. Whose heart are you helping to keep soft while it is still called Today, and who is charged with keeping yours?

_meta.title: Consider Jesus, Greater than Moses
reflection: The writer bids us "consider Jesus, the Apostle and High Priest of our confession" (v.1). Where do you still look for a word from God, or a way to God, apart from him?
We are Christ's house, and partakers of Christ, "if we hold fast" our confidence firm to the end (v.6; v.14). Is your assurance resting on a past profession, or on a confidence you are holding today?
"Exhort one another daily, while it is called To day, lest any of you be hardened through the deceitfulness of sin" (v.13). Whose heart are you helping to keep soft, and who is watching over yours?
